phases.label: Voter Registration
phases.description: Ensure you are on the National Register of Electors.
steps: Update address if you have moved
voterActions: Register at least 6 days before polling day
Check if you are registered at your current address

phases.label: Candidate Nomination
phases.description: Candidates file their nomination papers to run for office.
steps: Submit nomination form to Returning Officer
voterActions: Review candidate profiles in your riding

phases.label: Election Day
phases.description: The final day to cast your ballot in person.
steps: Go to your assigned polling station
Bring proof of identity and address
Mark your ballot behind the screen
voterActions: Check your voter information card for location
Ensure you have accepted forms of ID

glossary.definition: An individual elected to represent one of the 338 electoral districts in the House of Commons.
Informal term for an electoral district or constituency in Canada.
The elected lower house of Canada's Parliament, consisting of 338 members.
The appointed upper house of Parliament. Senators are appointed by the Governor General on the advice of the Prime Minister.
The electoral system where the candidate with the most votes in a riding wins the seat.
The representative of the Monarch in Canada, who performs formal constitutional duties like dissolving Parliament.
A formal document issued to a Returning Officer ordering that an election be held.
The person responsible for managing a candidate's campaign finances.
The independent agency responsible for conducting federal elections and referendums.
The individual who heads Elections Canada and reports directly to Parliament.
When the governing party holds more than half of the 338 seats in the House of Commons.
When the governing party has the most seats but fewer than 170, requiring support from other parties to pass laws.
A vote that tests whether the government still has the support of the House of Commons.
The opportunity to vote in person before Election Day at designated locations.
A method of voting by mail or at an Elections Canada office, available to any eligible voter.
A card sent to registered voters telling them when and where to vote.
A person appointed by a candidate to observe the voting and counting of ballots.
The list of all registered voters in a riding.

quickQuestions: How many MPs are in Canada?
Do I need ID to vote in Canada?

quiz.question: How many seats are currently in the Canadian House of Commons?
quiz.explanation: There are currently 338 electoral districts, each electing one MP.
quiz.funFact: The number of seats is adjusted after each decennial census to reflect population changes.

quiz.question: What is the common term for an electoral district in Canada?
quiz.explanation: 'Riding' is a traditional term for a federal or provincial electoral district.
quiz.funFact: The term 'riding' comes from an Old English word 'thriding', meaning a third part.

quiz.question: Who is eligible to vote in a Canadian federal election?
quiz.explanation: To vote federally, you must be a Canadian citizen and at least 18 years old on election day.
quiz.funFact: Since 2019, Canadian citizens living abroad can also vote, regardless of how long they've been away.

quiz.question: What happens if the government loses a 'vote of confidence'?
options: The PM is fired but the party stays
The government usually falls, leading to an election
quiz.explanation: Under the principle of responsible government, a government that loses a confidence vote must either resign or ask for an election.
quiz.funFact: Budget votes are always considered matters of confidence.

quiz.question: How is the Prime Minister of Canada chosen?
options: The leader of the party with the most seats in the House
Chosen by the Governor General randomly
quiz.explanation: The Governor General invites the leader of the party most likely to hold the 'confidence' of the House (usually the party with the most seats) to become PM.
quiz.funFact: Technically, the PM is appointed by the Governor General, but by constitutional convention, it's always the party leader.

quiz.question: What is the maximum duration of a federal election campaign in Canada?
quiz.explanation: The Canada Elections Act sets the maximum campaign length at 50 days.
quiz.funFact: The minimum campaign length is 36 days. The 2015 election was one of the longest in history at 78 days, before the 50-day limit was introduced.
